senses.definition: A medical imaging test that uses X-rays and contrast dye to show the inside of arteries.
examples: The doctor ordered coronary arteriography to look for blockages in her heart arteries.
Arteriography can help find narrow or damaged arteries before serious problems occur.

senses.definition: Relating to or involving arterioles, the very small branches of arteries.
examples: The biopsy showed thickening of the arteriolar walls.
Arteriolar changes are common in long-term high blood pressure.

senses.definition: A very small artery that leads to capillaries.
examples: Arterioles help control blood pressure by tightening or relaxing.
Damage to the kidney arterioles can affect how well the kidneys work.

senses.definition: Plural of arteriole: very small arteries that lead to capillaries.
examples: The arterioles narrow to reduce blood flow to the skin in cold weather.
High blood pressure can damage arterioles throughout the body.

senses.definition: A small calcified mass or stone formed in the wall of an arteriole.
examples: Microscopy revealed an arteriolith within the vessel wall.
Arterioliths may appear as tiny hard spots in diseased microvessels.

senses.definition: The scientific study of arteries and their diseases.
examples: Her research in arteriology focuses on early changes in the artery wall.
Arteriology overlaps with cardiology and vascular surgery.

senses.definition: Plural of arteriolosclerosis: conditions where the walls of arterioles become thick and hard.
examples: Long-standing diabetes can lead to various arterioloscleroses.
Hypertensive arterioloscleroses affect small arteries in many organs.

senses.definition: Hardening and thickening of the walls of very small arteries called arterioles.
examples: Arteriolosclerosis is a common finding in elderly patients with high blood pressure.
Diabetes can speed up arteriolosclerosis in the kidneys.

senses.definition: Softening or loss of firmness in the wall of an artery.
examples: Arteriomalacia may increase the risk of artery rupture.
The pathologist reported arteriomalacia in the affected vessel.

senses.definition: An instrument used to measure the diameter of arteries while blood flows through them.
examples: The researcher used an arteriometer to record changes in vessel size.
An arteriometer can detect subtle narrowing of peripheral arteries.

senses.definition: Controlling or affecting the movement or tone of artery walls.
examples: Arteriomotor nerves regulate vessel constriction and dilation.
The drug has a direct arteriomotor effect.

senses.definition: Death of tissue in the wall of an artery.
examples: Severe inflammation led to arterionecrosis in the affected limb.
The biopsy showed arterionecrosis with rupture of the vessel.

senses.definition: A noticeable throbbing or strong pulse in an artery.
examples: Arteriopalmus in the neck may worry patients after exercise.
The doctor explained that mild arteriopalmus can be harmless.

senses.definition: Any disease that affects the arteries.
examples: Diabetic arteriopathy can damage the blood supply to the feet.
The patient showed signs of widespread arteriopathy.

senses.definition: A surgical operation that joins an artery to a vein or involves cutting both an artery and a vein.
examples: An arteriophlebotomy was performed to create a dialysis fistula.
The report described an arteriophlebotomy between the radial artery and a nearby vein.

senses.definition: An abnormal course or path of an artery.
examples: Arterioplania of the coronary artery can complicate heart surgery.
Imaging revealed arterioplania with the vessel looping behind the organ.

senses.definition: Surgical repair or reconstruction of an artery.
examples: Arterioplasty was needed to restore blood flow to the leg.
The surgeon performed arterioplasty using a vein graft.

senses.definition: A substance or drug that raises arterial blood pressure.
examples: An arteriopressor was given to stabilize the patient’s blood pressure.
The ICU team adjusted the arteriopressor dose carefully.

senses.definition: Relating to both the arteries and the kidneys, or to the arteries of the kidneys.
examples: Arteriorenal disease can lead to difficult-to-control blood pressure.
They studied arteriorenal changes in patients with long-term hypertension.

senses.definition: Severe or active bleeding from an artery.
examples: The trauma caused massive arteriorrhagia from the thigh.
Prompt control of arteriorrhagia is vital in major injuries.

senses.definition: Surgical stitching or suturing of a cut or injured artery.
examples: Arteriorrhaphy was performed to repair the lacerated vessel.
Timely arteriorrhaphy can save a limb after trauma.

senses.definition: Tearing or rupture of an artery.
examples: Sudden arteriorrhexis can cause life-threatening bleeding.
The aneurysm ended in arteriorrhexis during exertion.

senses.definition: Plural of arteriosclerosis: several conditions involving hardening and thickening of artery walls.
examples: Different arterioscleroses affect large and small arteries in various ways.
The study compared the three main arterioscleroses.

senses.definition: A general term for hardening and loss of flexibility in the walls of arteries.
examples: Arteriosclerosis becomes more common as people get older.
Lifestyle changes can slow down arteriosclerosis.

senses.definition: Relating to or caused by arteriosclerosis.
examples: The scan showed arteriosclerotic changes in the brain vessels.
Arteriosclerotic heart disease is a leading cause of death.

senses.definition: People who suffer from arteriosclerosis.
examples: Dietary advice for arteriosclerotics focuses on reducing fat and salt.
The clinic provides support for arteriosclerotics and their families.

senses.definition: A sudden tightening of an artery that reduces its diameter.
examples: Arteriospasm can temporarily cut off blood flow to the brain.
Cold exposure may trigger arteriospasm in some patients.

senses.definition: Narrowing of an artery.
examples: Arteriostenosis of the carotid artery can increase stroke risk.
The scan revealed arteriostenosis at several points.

senses.definition: Abnormal hardening or bone-like change in an artery wall.
examples: Advanced arteriostosis may be seen in long-standing vascular disease.
The vessel showed arteriostosis with extensive calcification.

senses.definition: A twisting or turning of an artery.
examples: Arteriostrepsis of the mesenteric vessels can reduce blood supply to the gut.
The surgeon corrected arteriostrepsis during the procedure.

senses.definition: Surgical cutting or removal of sympathetic nerves to an artery to change its tone or blood flow.
examples: Arteriosympathectomy was once used to treat severe limb ischemia.
The report described lumbar arteriosympathectomy for pain relief.

senses.definition: A surgical instrument used to cut into an artery.
examples: The surgeon selected a fine arteriotome for the delicate vessel.
An arteriotome is designed to make controlled incisions in artery walls.

senses.definition: Plural of arteriotomy: surgical cuts made into arteries.
examples: Multiple arteriotomies were needed during the bypass operation.
The surgeon carefully closed the arteriotomies with fine sutures.

senses.definition: A surgical cut made into an artery.
examples: An arteriotomy was performed to remove the clot.
The arteriotomy allowed access to the blocked segment.

senses.definition: A crushing or pressuring of an artery, especially as a treatment.
examples: Arteriotrepsis may be used to stop bleeding from small vessels.
The historical text mentioned arteriotrepsis as an early surgical method.

senses.definition: Relating to an artery or arteries.
examples: The sample was taken from arterious blood.
Arterious circulation carries oxygen-rich blood from the heart.

senses.definition: Relating to or connecting both arteries and veins.
examples: An arteriovenous malformation can cause serious bleeding in the brain.
The patient needed an arteriovenous fistula for dialysis.

senses.definition: A procedure that changes the rhythm of the heart by acting on the arteries or arterial system.
examples: The case report mentioned experimental arterioversion techniques.
Arterioversion aims to restore more normal blood flow patterns.

senses.definition: A device used to perform arterioversion or to adjust arterial or heart rhythm.
examples: The new arterioverter was tested in animal models.
Engineers designed an implantable arterioverter for high-risk patients.

senses.definition: Plural of arteritis: inflammatory diseases of the arteries.
examples: Different arteritides can affect patients of various ages.
The review covered large-vessel and small-vessel arteritides.

senses.definition: Inflammation of the wall of an artery.
examples: Temporal arteritis can cause sudden loss of vision.
The doctor suspected arteritis due to raised inflammatory markers.

senses.definition: Plural of arteritis: different conditions that involve inflammation of arteries.
examples: Various arteritises share similar symptoms such as fever and pain.
The textbook lists several arteritises that affect young adults.

senses.definition: A blood vessel that carries blood away from the heart to the rest of the body.
examples: The surgeon cleared a blockage in the main artery to his leg.
Smoking can damage the arteries over time.

senses.definition: A main road or route that is important for traffic or movement.
examples: This highway is a major artery into the city.
The protest blocked a main traffic artery for hours.

senses.definition: Acting as a main route or channel like an artery.
examples: A wide boulevard arteries the new district from north to south.
The canal arteries goods through the industrial zone.

senses.definition: Relating to a well where water rises to the surface under its own pressure.
examples: They drilled an artesian well to supply the farm with water.
Artesian water flows naturally without pumping.

senses.definition: A richly decorated coffered wooden ceiling, especially in Spanish Mudéjar architecture.
examples: The palace hall is famous for its carved artesonado.
Visitors admired the painted artesonado above their heads.

senses.definition: Plural of artesonado: several decorative coffered wooden ceilings.
examples: The building preserves some of the finest artesonados in the region.
Museums often show photos of historic artesonados.

senses.definition: Showing skill and creativity, especially in art or design.
examples: The film makes artful use of light and shadow.
She gave an artful performance in the play.

senses.definition: Good at using clever or indirect methods to get what you want, sometimes in a dishonest way.
examples: With an artful question, he avoided answering directly.
The ad is an artful attempt to influence voters.

senses.definition: In a skilful and creative way.
examples: The room was artfully decorated with local crafts.
The director artfully blends comedy and drama.

senses.definition: In a clever, indirect, or slightly dishonest way.
examples: She artfully changed the subject.
The company artfully avoids using the word "fees".

senses.definition: The quality of being skilful and creative, especially in art or design.
examples: Critics praised the artfulness of the novel’s structure.
Her cooking shows great artfulness in combining flavors.

senses.definition: The quality of being clever at getting what you want, often by indirect or slightly dishonest methods.
examples: He handled the negotiation with great artfulness.
Her apparent innocence hides real artfulness.
